meta.title: Social Networks Security Interactive Lab — Data Store
meta.author: Dr. Ali Salim
meta.description: Question bank and reference data for the SNS Security Lab backend

trustedSeedSites.label: Wikipedia
trustedSeedSites.reason: Highly cited, community-maintained, non-commercial knowledge base

trustedSeedSites.label: National Institutes of Health
trustedSeedSites.reason: Official US federal health agency; peer-reviewed publication host

trustedSeedSites.label: IEEE
trustedSeedSites.reason: Largest technical professional organisation; rigorous peer-review process

trustedSeedSites.label: ACM Digital Library
trustedSeedSites.reason: Premier CS academic publisher; all papers peer-reviewed

trustedSeedSites.label: Google Scholar
trustedSeedSites.reason: Indexes peer-reviewed literature across all disciplines

trustedSeedSites.label: BBC News
trustedSeedSites.reason: Publicly funded broadcaster; strict editorial guidelines and fact-checking

trustedSeedSites.label: Reuters
trustedSeedSites.reason: International news agency with 170+ year track record; non-partisan editorial policy

trustedSeedSites.label: Snopes
trustedSeedSites.reason: Dedicated fact-checking organisation; transparent sourcing methodology

trustedSeedSites.label: Internet Archive
trustedSeedSites.reason: Non-profit digital library; preserves web content for historical reference

trustedSeedSites.label: MITRE CVE
trustedSeedSites.reason: US federally-funded CVE database; authoritative source for vulnerability data

trustedSeedSites.label: NIST
trustedSeedSites.reason: US standards body; publishes authoritative cybersecurity frameworks (NIST CSF)

trustedSeedSites.label: OWASP
trustedSeedSites.reason: Open non-profit web security community; OWASP Top 10 is industry standard

questions.q: What is the term for intelligence produced by collecting and analyzing publicly available information?
questions.exp: OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) is defined as intelligence produced by collecting, evaluating, and analyzing publicly available information with the purpose of answering a specific intelligence question.

questions.q: In a Sybil attack, what is the "Second Hop"?
opts: Friends of friends — carrying Inherited Social Trust
Direct friends of the fake account
questions.exp: The Second Hop refers to Friends of Friends. When a fake account reaches this level, it gains "Inherited Social Trust" because targets see a mutual friend from the First Hop and assume legitimacy.

questions.q: Which element in a photograph is described as a "wealth signal" by the lecture?
opts: A high-end smartphone or designer clothing
The number of likes on the post
The color filter applied to the image
questions.exp: High-end smartphones, specific watch brands, or designer clothing are "wealth signals" that make individuals targets for social engineering and targeted financial attacks.

questions.q: What does the damping factor (d=0.85) represent in PageRank and TrustRank?
opts: Decrease importance of all pages equally
Probability that a random surfer continues clicking links
questions.exp: The damping factor d (typically 0.85) models the probability that a random surfer continues following links rather than "teleporting" to a new page, preventing rank sinks and ensuring convergence.

questions.q: Which data category includes GPS coordinates from posts and IP addresses?
questions.exp: Location Data encompasses GPS coordinates from posts, IP addresses, and check-ins — critical privacy concerns since this data can be used for stalking and criminal activities.

questions.q: What is the Levenshtein distance between "facebook" and "faceb00k"?
questions.exp: Two substitutions are required: replacing the first "o" with "0" and the second "o" with "0". Similarity = 1 - (2/8) = 75%, which exceeds the danger threshold.

questions.q: What is "Credential Stuffing"?
opts: Creating unique passwords for every site
Using stolen credentials from one site to breach accounts on other platforms
questions.exp: Credential Stuffing exploits password reuse. Attackers take username/password pairs stolen from a breached site and systematically test them against other services like banks and social media.

questions.q: Which malware type locks user devices and demands payment for restored access?
questions.exp: Ransomware encrypts a user's files or locks their device and demands payment to restore access. Social media is frequently used to distribute ransomware download links.

questions.q: Which platform is classified as a Microblogging Service?
questions.exp: X (formerly Twitter) is a microblogging platform enabling real-time public conversation, news broadcasting, and short-form text communication.

questions.q: Which OSINT technique uses shadow angle and length to determine when a photo was taken?
questions.exp: Chronolocation analyzes shadow angles against sun position data using tools like SunCalc to determine the exact date and time a photograph was taken.

questions.q: Which graph metric — measuring whether your friends also know each other — detects fake networks?
questions.exp: The Clustering Coefficient measures how much nodes cluster together. Real networks are "incestuous" — your friends know each other. Bot networks lack cross-links, producing a suspiciously low coefficient.

questions.q: In TrustRank, where does the "random browser" always teleport back to?
opts: Any random page on the web
A page from the manually verified Trusted Seed Set S
questions.exp: TrustRank restricts the teleportation set to only trusted seed pages S. This biases the random walk toward trusted content, ensuring pages far from seeds receive low trust scores.

questions.q: What is EXIF data and what security risk does it pose?
opts: Extended File Info — reveals software version only
Exchangeable Image File Format — embeds GPS, timestamp, device info in photos
External Index Format — tracks social media engagement
Encrypted Exchange Format — secures image transmission
questions.exp: EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) is metadata embedded in photos containing GPS coordinates, timestamp, camera model, and device information — all usable to locate or track users.

questions.q: What attack exploits public café charging stations to install malware on devices?
questions.exp: Juice Jacking occurs when attackers compromise public USB charging stations to install malware or steal data via the charging cable when users plug in.

questions.q: Why does fake news typically spread faster than factual information on social networks?
opts: Fake news uses better quality images
Novel, emotionally triggering content has a higher transmission rate and gets shared more
Verified accounts always share fake news first
questions.exp: Research confirms fake news spreads faster because it tends to be more novel and emotionally charged — triggering anger, fear, or surprise, which significantly increases sharing behavior.

questions.q: What is the key difference between PageRank and TrustRank?
opts: TrustRank uses a higher damping factor
PageRank only works on social networks
TrustRank restricts teleportation to a manually verified seed set instead of all pages
questions.exp: The core difference: PageRank teleports to any random page with equal probability. TrustRank restricts teleportation exclusively to a small, manually verified Trusted Seed Set S, biasing propagation toward trusted content.

questions.q: What is a Sybil Attack in the context of social networks?
opts: A brute-force password attack against user accounts
Creating large numbers of fake identities to gain disproportionate influence
Injecting malicious scripts into social media posts
Stealing session cookies to hijack accounts
questions.exp: A Sybil Attack involves creating many fake accounts (Sybil nodes) to gain disproportionate influence, spread misinformation, manipulate votes, or deceive users by faking social credibility.

questions.q: Which tool is specifically designed for shadow-based chronolocation of photographs?
questions.exp: SunCalc is a solar position calculator that, when combined with a known location, allows analysts to determine the exact time a photograph was taken by matching shadow angles to sun position data.

questions.q: What are homoglyph substitutions in phishing attacks?
opts: Creating identical copies of login forms
Replacing letters with visually similar characters (e.g., 'o'→'0', 'l'→'1')
Generating random subdomains to confuse users
Using foreign-language domains to bypass filters
questions.exp: Homoglyph substitutions replace standard characters with visually similar ones — 'paypa1.com' instead of 'paypal.com'. The Levenshtein distance detects these with a small edit distance and high similarity score.

questions.q: Which security measure significantly reduces account takeover risk even when passwords are stolen?
opts: Using a different username on every platform
questions.exp: Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) adds a second verification layer — typically a time-based OTP or SMS code — that an attacker cannot access even with the correct password, blocking credential stuffing and phishing.
